subject: Chemistry
q: KMnO4 is titrated with ferrous ammonium sulphate hexahydrate in presence of dilute H2SO4. Number of water molecules produced for 2 molecules of KMnO4 is _______
question: KMnO4 is titrated with ferrous ammonium sulphate hexahydrate in presence of dilute H2SO4. Number of water molecules produced for 2 molecules of KMnO4 is _______
solution:
By balancing redox reaction:

10[FeSO4.(NH4)2SO4.6H2O]+2KMNO4+8H2SO4→5Fe2(SO4)3+2MnSO4+10(NH4)2SO4+K2SO4+68H2O

8H++MnO4-+5Fe+2⟶5Fe+3+Mn+2+4H2O

For 2 moles of MnO4-, 8 moles of H2O. are released 5 moles of Mohr's salt contain 30 moles of H2O. So, 10 moles of Mohr's salt contain 60 moles of H2O So, total moles of H2O produced=68

q:
Given:
Co3++e-→Co2+;E°=+1.81V
Pb3++2e-→Pb2+;E°=+1.67V
Ce4++e-→Ce3+;E°=+1.61V
Bi3++3e-→Bi;E°=+0.20V
Oxidizing power of the species will increase in the order:
question:
Given:
Co3++e-→Co2+;E°=+1.81V
Pb3++2e-→Pb2+;E°=+1.67V
Ce4++e-→Ce3+;E°=+1.61V
Bi3++3e-→Bi;E°=+0.20V
Oxidizing power of the species will increase in the order:
solution:
Higher the value of ERed0; higher will be its oxidizing power.

Hence correct order is Bi+3<Ce+4<Pb+4<Co+3

question:

solution:
VNaOH=4.4 ml

eq. of NaOH= eq. of H2C2O4

or, M×4.4×1=1.25×10×2

or, M=5.68M

∴ Nearest integer answer is 6.

q:
Match the LIST-I with LIST-II
Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
Match the LIST-I with LIST-II
Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
solution:
(A) $\mathrm{CH}_4(\mathrm{~g})+2 \mathrm{O}_2(\mathrm{~g}) \xrightarrow{\Delta} 2 \mathrm{CO}_2(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l}) \Rightarrow$ combination reaction
(B) 2 NaH (s) $\xrightarrow{\Delta} 2 \mathrm{Na}(\mathrm{s}) \quad+\mathrm{H}_2(\mathrm{~g}) \quad \Rightarrow$ Decomposition reaction
(C) $\mathrm{V}_2 \mathrm{O}_5(\mathrm{~s})+5 \mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{s}) \xrightarrow{\Delta} 2 \mathrm{~V}(\mathrm{~s})+5 \mathrm{CaO}(\mathrm{s})$ $\Rightarrow$ Displacement reaction
(D) $2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}_2$ (aq.) $\xrightarrow{\Delta} 2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})+\mathrm{O}_2(\mathrm{~g})$ $\Rightarrow$ Disproportionation reaction

q: While titration dilute <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>HCI</mi></math> solution with aqueous <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>NaOH</mi></math>, which of the following will not be required ?
question: While titration dilute <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>HCI</mi></math> solution with aqueous <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>NaOH</mi></math>, which of the following will not be required ?
solution:
In this acid base Titrating there is no use of Bunsen burner and measuring cylinder other laboratory equipments will be required for getting the end point of titration.

These are the equipment required for titration.

  • Burette
  • Conical flask
  • Beaker
  • Pipette
  • Stirring rod
  • Small (filter) funnel
  • Burette stand and clamp (note 2)
  • White tile 
  • Indicator

subject: Chemistry
q:
Given below are two statements: One is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R

Assertion A : Permanganate titration are not performed in presence of hydrochloric acid.

Reason R : Chlorine is formed as a consequence of oxidation of hydrochloric acid.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below
question:
Given below are two statements: One is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R

Assertion A : Permanganate titration are not performed in presence of hydrochloric acid.

Reason R : Chlorine is formed as a consequence of oxidation of hydrochloric acid.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below
options: Both <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> are true and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> is the correct explanation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>

Both <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> are true but <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> is NOT the correct explanation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>
<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> is true but <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> is false
<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> is false but <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> is true
solution:
Hydrochloric acid (HCl) is usually not used in the process of titration because it reacts with the indicator potassium permanganate (KMnO4) that is used in the process. It reacts with KMnO4 solution and gets oxidized which further results in the liberation of chlorine gas according to the following reaction.

2KMnO4+16HCl→2MnCl2+2KCl+8H2O+5Cl2

So, Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A

subject: Chemistry
q: The density of a monobasic strong acid (Molar mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>24</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo> </mo><mi>mol</mi><mo>)</mo></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>21</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi></math>. The volume of its solution required for the complete neutralization of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>24</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mo> </mo><mi>NaOH</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi></math> (Nearest integer)
question: The density of a monobasic strong acid (Molar mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>24</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo> </mo><mi>mol</mi><mo>)</mo></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>21</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi></math>. The volume of its solution required for the complete neutralization of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>24</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mo> </mo><mi>NaOH</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi></math> (Nearest integer)
solution:
millimole of NaOH=0.24×25
For nuetralisation, equal moles of monoacidic base and monobasic acid must be present:

∴ millimole of acid =0.24×25

⇒ mass of acid =0.24×25×24.2 g

for pure acid,

V=wd;(d=1.21 kg/L=1.21 g/ml)

∴V=0.24×25×24.21.12×10-3

=120×10-3ml

=12×10-2ml

subject: Chemistry
q: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>02</mn><msub><mi>MK</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>Cr</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>7</mn></msub></math> solution is used for the titration of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>Fe</mi><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup></math> solution in the acidic medium. The molarity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>Fe</mi><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup></math> solution is____<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></math>
question: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>02</mn><msub><mi>MK</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>Cr</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>7</mn></msub></math> solution is used for the titration of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>Fe</mi><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup></math> solution in the acidic medium. The molarity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>Fe</mi><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup></math> solution is____<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></math>
solution:
Equivalents of  K2Cr2O7= Equivalents of Fe2+

⇒(Molarity × volume × n.f) of K2Cr2O7= (molarity × volume × n.f) of Fe2+

n.f=n-factor(valence factor)

⇒0.02×20×6=M×10×1

⇒M=0.24

⇒ Molarity =24×10-2

subject: Chemistry
q: Which of the given reactions is not an example of disproportionation reaction?
question: Which of the given reactions is not an example of disproportionation reaction?
solution:
Disproportionation reaction, is a type of redox reaction involving simultaneous reduction and oxidation of atoms of the same element from one oxidation state (OS) to two different oxidation states.

2H2O2-1⟶2H2O-2+O20 : Disproportionation

2NO2+H2O→HNO3+HNO2 : Disproportionation

MnO4-+4H++3e-→MnO2+2H2O : reduction only

3MnO42-+4H+→2MnO4-+MnO2+4+2H2O : Disproportionation

subject: Chemistry
q:
Which of the following options are correct for the reaction?

2Au(CN)2-(aq)+Zn(s)→2Au(s)+Zn(CN)42-(aq)

A. Redox reaction

B. Displacement reaction

C. Decomposition reaction

D. Combination reaction

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
Which of the following options are correct for the reaction?

2Au(CN)2-(aq)+Zn(s)→2Au(s)+Zn(CN)42-(aq)

A. Redox reaction

B. Displacement reaction

C. Decomposition reaction

D. Combination reaction

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
solution:
Gold is then extracted from this complex by displacement technique by using a more electropositive zinc metal.

In this method, zinc (Zn) acts as a reducing agent and it reduces Au + to Au and itself gets oxidized from Zn to Zn+2 ions which combine with CN- ions and form soluble complex, sodium tetracyanozincate (II).

2Au(CN)2- aq+Zn s→2Au s+Zn(CN)42- aq

subject: Chemistry
q:
How many electrons are involved in the following redox reaction ?

    Cr 2 O 7 2 - + Fe 2 + + C 2 O 4 2 - → Cr 3 + + Fe 3 + + CO 2  (Unbalanced)
question:
How many electrons are involved in the following redox reaction ?

    Cr 2 O 7 2 - + Fe 2 + + C 2 O 4 2 - → Cr 3 + + Fe 3 + + CO 2  (Unbalanced)
solution:
[F e 2+ + C 2 O 4 2−   →  F e 3+ +2C O 2 +3 e − ]×2(oxidation)
C r 2 O 7 2− +14 H + +6 e −   →  2C r 3+ +7 H 2 O(reduction)
_____________________________________________________

Cr2O72−+2Fe 2++2C2O42−+14H+→2Cr3++2Fe3++4CO2+7H2O

Hence, total 6 electrons are involved in the net redox reaction to happen.

subject: Chemistry
q: One mole of $\mathrm{Cl}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$ was passed into 2 L of cold 2 M KOH solution. After the reaction, the concentrations of $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}, \mathrm{ClO}^{-}$and $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$are respectively (assume volume remains constant)
question: One mole of $\mathrm{Cl}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$ was passed into 2 L of cold 2 M KOH solution. After the reaction, the concentrations of $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}, \mathrm{ClO}^{-}$and $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$are respectively (assume volume remains constant)
solution:
Cl₂ undergoes disproportionation in cold KOH: $Cl_2 + 2OH^- \rightarrow Cl^- + ClO^- + H_2O$
Initial: 1 mole Cl₂, 2 L × 2 M KOH = 4 moles OH⁻
Molar ratio Cl₂:OH⁻ = 1:2 (stoichiometric)
Products formed: 1 mole Cl⁻, 1 mole ClO⁻; Excess OH⁻ = 4 - 2 = 2 moles
In 2 L solution: [Cl⁻] = 0.5 M, [ClO⁻] = 0.5 M, [OH⁻] = 1 M

subject: Chemistry
q:
Consider the reaction :

H 2 SO 3 aq + Sn aq 4 + + H 2 O l⁡ → Sn aq 2 + + HSO 4 aq - + 3 H aq +

Which of the following statements is correct ?
question:
Consider the reaction :

H 2 SO 3 aq + Sn aq 4 + + H 2 O l⁡ → Sn aq 2 + + HSO 4 aq - + 3 H aq +

Which of the following statements is correct ?
options: Sn<sup>4</sup><sup>+</sup> is the oxidizing agent because it undergoes oxidation
Sn<sup>4</sup><sup>+</sup> is the reducing agent because it undergoes oxidation
H<sub>2</sub>SO<sub>3</sub> is the reducing agent because it undergoes reduction
H<sub>2</sub>SO<sub>3</sub> is the reducing agent because it undergoes oxidation
solution:
Identify oxidation states of all elements in Redox Reaction

H2SO+43+Sn4++H2O(l)→Sn2++HSO4- +6 +3H+

S is changing the oxidation state from (+4) to (+6). i.e H2SO3 is a Reducing agent because it undergoes oxidation.

subject: Chemistry
q: The normality of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>SO</mi><mn>4</mn></msub></math> in the solution obtained on mixing <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mo> </mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>SO</mi><mn>4</mn></msub></math> with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mo> </mo><mi>NaOH</mi></math> is____<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math>.
question: The normality of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>SO</mi><mn>4</mn></msub></math> in the solution obtained on mixing <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mo> </mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>SO</mi><mn>4</mn></msub></math> with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mo> </mo><mi>NaOH</mi></math> is____<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math>.
solution:
No. of equivalents of H2SO4=M×V×n-factor = 100×0.1×2=20

No. of equivalents of NaOH=50×0.1=5

No. of equivalents of H2SO4 left =20-5=15

⇒150×x=15

x=110=0.1 N=1×10-1 N

subject: Chemistry
q: During the reaction of permanganate with thiosulphate, the change in oxidation of manganese occurs by value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math>. Identify which of the below medium will favour the reaction.
question: During the reaction of permanganate with thiosulphate, the change in oxidation of manganese occurs by value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math>. Identify which of the below medium will favour the reaction.
options: Both aqueous acidic and faintly alkaline
solution:
In neutral or weakly alkaline solution oxidation state of Mn changes by 3 unit. The reaction between permanganate and thiosulfate is a redox reaction, in which permanganate acts as an oxidizing agent and thiosulfate acts as a reducing agent.

MnO4⊝+S2O3-2→MnO2+SO4-2

This ionic mechanism is favoured in neutral aqueous medium.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Given below are two statements:
Statement I: Sodium dichromate and potassium dichromate are classified as primary standards in titrimetric analysis.
Statement II: Phenolphthalein is a weak base, therefore it dissociates in acidic medium.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below
question:
Given below are two statements:
Statement I: Sodium dichromate and potassium dichromate are classified as primary standards in titrimetric analysis.
Statement II: Phenolphthalein is a weak base, therefore it dissociates in acidic medium.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below
options: Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Statement I is false but Statement II is true
solution:
Statement I is false because sodium dichromate is deliquescent (absorbs moisture from the atmosphere) and cannot be weighed accurately, so it is not used as a primary standard. Potassium dichromate, however, is a primary standard.

Statement II is false because phenolphthalein is a weak organic acid, not a weak base. It remains largely undissociated in an acidic medium (colorless) and dissociates in a basic medium to give a pink color.

Therefore, both Statement I and Statement II are false.

Answer: Both Statement I and Statement II are false

subject: Chemistry
q: An example of a disproportionation reaction is
question: An example of a disproportionation reaction is
options: <math><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">u</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi><mo>→</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">u</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mo>+</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">u</mi></math>

<math><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi><mo>+</mo><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mo>→</mo><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi><mo>+</mo><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math>
solution:
Disproportionation reaction: In this type of reaction, same elemental species gets oxidised as well as reduced.

 Na+1Br-1+Cl20→2Na+1Cl-1+Br20-not disproportionate,

2KMn+7O4→K2Mn+6O4+Mn+4O2+O2-not disproportionate,

2Mn+7O4-+10J-+16H+→2Mn+2+SI2+8H2O-not disproportionate
 

-It is disproportionate.

subject: Chemistry
q: The volume of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>02</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></math> aqueous <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>HBr</mi></math> required to neutralize <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>01</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></math> aqueous <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Ba</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>OH</mi><msub><mo>)</mo><mn>2</mn></msub></math> is (Assume complete neutralization)
question: The volume of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>02</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></math> aqueous <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>HBr</mi></math> required to neutralize <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>01</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></math> aqueous <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Ba</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>OH</mi><msub><mo>)</mo><mn>2</mn></msub></math> is (Assume complete neutralization)
solution:
The balanced chemical equation for the reaction between HBr and Ba(OH)2 is:

2HBr+Ba(OH)2 → BaBr2+2H2O

From this equation, we can see that 2 moles of HBr react with 1 mole of Ba(OH)2.

Equal equivalents will react. 

Number of equivalents = Normality ×Volume

So,

N1V1=N2V20.02×V1=0.02×10V1=10 ml

subject: Chemistry
q:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion (A) and the other is labelled as Reason (R).

Assertion (A) : An aqueous solution of KOH when for volumetric analysis, its concentration should be checked before the use.

Reason (R) : On aging,KOH solution absorbs atmospheric CO2.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below.
question:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion (A) and the other is labelled as Reason (R).

Assertion (A) : An aqueous solution of KOH when for volumetric analysis, its concentration should be checked before the use.

Reason (R) : On aging,KOH solution absorbs atmospheric CO2.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below.
options: (A) is not correct but (R) is correct
Both (A) and (R) are correct but (R) is not the correct explanation of (A)
Both (A) and (R) are correct and (R) is the correct explanation of (A)
(A) is correct but (R) is not correct
solution:
KOH absorb CO2 based on the following reaction:
CO2(g) + 2KOH(aq) → K2CO3(aq) + H2O(l)
Thus, concentration of Carbon dioxide decreases over time, prior to volumetric analysis concentration of potassium hydroxide should be checked.

vf=1  vf=5

subject: Chemistry
q: The compound that cannot act both as oxidizing and reducing agent is
question: The compound that cannot act both as oxidizing and reducing agent is
solution:
In HNO2, Nitrogen has oxidation state +3, nitrogen has maximum oxidation state +5 and minimum -3, hence nitrous acid can act both as oxidizing and reducing agent.

Sulphur has maximum oxidation state +6 and minimum is -2, in H2SO3 sulphur has oxidation state +4, hence it can act as both oxidizing and reducing agent.

For oxygen maximum oxidation state is +2 and minimum is -2, in H2O2 Oxygen has oxidation state -1, hence it can act bot as oxidizing and reducing agent.

As in H3PO4, Phosphorous is present in it’s maximum oxidation state i.e., +5, hence it cannot act as reducing agent.
